title-group.article-title: Structural Enzymology, Phylogenetics, Differentiation, and Symbolic Reflexivity at the Dawn of Biology

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.p: Translation of symbols in one chemical language into another defined genetics. Yet, the co-linearity of codons and amino acids is so commonplace an idea that few even ask how it arose. Readout is done by two distinct sets of proteins, called aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases must enforce the rules first used to assemble themselves. To understand the roots of translation, we must experimentally test the structural codes that the earliest aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases used to recognize both amino acid and RNA substrates. We present here new results on five different facets of that problem. (i) The surfaces of structures coded by opposite strands of the same gene have opposite polarities. Core residues in proteins from one strand are surface residues in proteins from the other strand. The complementarity of base pairing thus projects into the proteome. That leads in turn to contrasting amino acid and RNA substrate binding modes. (ii) Escherichia coli reproduces in vivo a nested hierarchy of active excerpts, or “urzymes,” similar to those we had designed as models for ancestral aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases. (iii) A third novel deletion produced in vivo and a new Class II urzyme suggest how to design bidirectional urzyme genes. (iv) Codon middle base pairing provides a basis to constrain Class I and II aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase family trees. (v) Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase urzymes acylate class-specific subsets of an RNA library, showing urzyme RNA substrate specificity for the first time. Four new tree-building tools augment these results to compose a viable platform for experimental study of the origins of genetic coding.

boxed-text.p: The origin of genetic coding poses questions distinct from those faced studying enzyme evolution since the first cells. Modern enzymes that translate the code range in size from ∼330 to ∼970 amino acids. Ancestral forms cannot have been nearly as complex. Moreover, such primitive enzymes likely could enforce only a much-reduced coding alphabet. Structural and molecular biology data point to a broad sketch of events leading to the code. That research platform will enable us to see how nature came to store information about the physical chemistry of amino acids in the coding table. That, in turn, allowed searching of a very broad amino acid sequence-structure space. Selection could then, concurrently, learn how to assemble amino acids into functional catalysts. Those catalysts had rates and fidelities consistent with bootstrapping the modern coding alphabet. New phylogenetic algorithms need to be developed to fully test that putative sketch experimentally.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Genetic coding requires a programming language (the codon table) and a set of programs written in that language to implement it. How did physical chemistry enable discovery about 4 billion years ago of the first genetic coding rules from among so many other possible sets? Those rules were needed to write blueprints into the sequences of the first genes whose translated products could then impose the same rules. The double helix (Watson and Crick 1953) inspired a series of brilliant studies. These quickly revealed details of the genetic coding table and the key roles of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (AARS)•tRNA cognate pairs (Hoagland et al. 1956; Berg and Offengand 1958; Nirenberg and Matthaei 1961; Trupin et al. 1965; Jones and Nirenberg 1966; Giegé 1972; Ostrem and Berg 1974).

Those rapid successes obscured questions about how the translation machinery might have arisen from the prebiotic world. For decades, inferences from such work remained as disconnected theories (Crick 1968; Yarus et al. 2009). Most (Koonin and Novozhilov 2017) ignored the crucial role played by AARS. As recently as 2000, and faced with very complex indications from the most recent phylogenetic analyses, an authoritative review by experts including specialists in the area of AARS began their conclusion as follows: “It is unlikely that the aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases played any specific role in the evolution of the genetic code; their evolutions did not shape the codon assignments” (Woese et al. 2000). Our view is just the opposite. Their statement is logically the same as if we were to proclaim that tRNA played no specific role in the evolution of the genetic code. Neither statement accounts for the way the present-day code relies solely on the specific fidelity of amino acid binding by AARS•tRNA cognate pairs. Both imply that it emerged from a precursory system that vanished without leaving a trace. That, in turn, would distinguish coding from virtually all other aspects of biology. The roots of coding must have left traces in modern biology just as everything else has done.

Translation requires a unique sequence of events. The first activates amino acids with ATP so that they can join together spontaneously. We showed that short, 50-residue fragments from both Class I and II AARS that we called “protozymes” from “proto” = first contains the ATP-binding site and accelerates amino acid activation by a factor of 106-fold (Martinez-Rodriguez et al. 2015; Onodera et al. 2021). The second links the amino acid covalently to tRNA. Doing so assigns a set of chemical symbols—the anticodons—to represent each side chain. That reaction can be catalyzed by somewhat longer, 80 to 130-residue constructs we call “urzymes” from “ur” = primordial, authentic. Although activation with ATP doubtless had other roles in prebiotic chemistry, it is hard to imagine any heritable genetic system without the second step, which makes the symbolic conversion. How did both activities arise within the same catalysts at the same time?

That question is closely tied to a related one. The readout from genes to catalytic function is done by proteins called AARS. The AARS gene products must read blueprints templated in the nucleotide sequences of their own genes. That AARS, collectively, can implement the rules first used to assemble themselves is a property we call reflexivity (Carter and Wills 2018b). What taught the ancestral AARS genes to enforce those assignment rules? We will understand the origins of reflexivity only when we can describe the earliest AARS•tRNA cognate pairs and the structural codes they used to recognize both amino acid and RNA substrates (Carter and Wills 2021b). Their rates and specificities must also support a cogent narrative that accounts for selection.

Recently, our collaborative teams have improved the early model systems and leveraged them into a story that matches structural changes to increasing function (Carter and Wills 2018a, 2018b, 2019a, 2019b, 2021a, 2021b; Wills and Carter 2018, 2020; Tang et al. 2023, 2024; Carter 2024; Patra et al. 2024). At the same time, we are enhancing those studies with new phylogenetics algorithms that sharpen the synthetase and tRNA family trees (Douglas et al. 2024a, 2024c, 2025b). From these and other studies, we sketch an answer to the rhetorical questions posed above. We summarize here a new paradigm for how nature discovered the coding rules by teaching a set of genes how to read their own blueprints.
That paradigm includes these elements.

list-item.p: The AARS are the central assignment catalysts necessary, along with their cognate tRNA molecules, to link amino acids specifically to their symbolic representations in all protein-coding genes. The earliest synthetase genes likely encoded Class I and II synthetases on opposite strands (Rodin and Ohno 1995).

The genetic code assigns nonpolar and polar amino acids to complementary codons. That gives protein sequences from bidirectional AARS genes inverted side chain polarity (Zull and Smith 1990; Carter 2024). Thus, amino acids that form the hydrophobic cores of proteins from one strand correspond to amino acids that form the surfaces of proteins from the other strand. The encoded Class I and II AARS pairs folded inside-out relative to one another into different 3D structures. That, in turn, led to different amino acid and RNA substrate binding modes (Carter and Wills 2019a, 2019b). These data thus root that initial substrate recognition in the base pairing between their coding sequences.

AARS urzymes actually prefer to acylate TΨC-minihelices, instead of full-length tRNAs (Tang et al. 2024). Both protein and RNA components therefore appear to have functioned first as simpler, single domains. Class I and II urzymes retain amino acid specificities consistent with enforcing a code of approximately four letters. Studies of RNA recognition confirm details of the code used by urzymes to recognize cognate minihelices (Carter and Wills 2018a, 2019a).

Catalysis by urzymes does not need active-site amino acids that were not present at early times (Tang et al. 2023). Phylogenetics and AI helped identify new AARS “urzymes” from novel sources (Patra et al. 2024). Escherichia coli creates in vivo a nested hierarchy of synthetase protozymes and urzymes similar to what it took two decades of analytical work to deconstruct.
To be successful, further work requires new phylogenetic algorithms to solve unique problems posed by the AARS family trees (Carter et al. 2022; Douglas et al. 2024a, 2024c).
A higher-level, reciprocally coupled gating shaped the energy landscape on which coding emerged (Carter and Wills 2021a).

sec.p: These aspects of our ongoing work now make a full platform for exploring the origins of genetics.

sec.title: Results
Synthetase Genes Must Interpret Their Own Blueprints
sec.p: AARS acquired a natural property that was novel and apparently unique in the universe so far known to us. They learned to read the instructions for their own assembly reflexively (Fig. 1), because it entails self-reference (Hofstadter 1979, 2007). Genetic coding stores the properties of the amino acid side chains—their size and polarity—(Fig. 1a) by matching them to a set of symbols (codons). To implement coding, nature then put that physical chemistry to use, discovering a set of gene sequences that initiated the reaction cycle shown by the broad red arrows in Fig. 1b. Peptides with those sequences could fold in water into 3D structures. Cavities in those 3D structures could recognize specific sets of amino acids and their corresponding symbols. They also could speed up forming a chemical bond between them. Remarkably, at the same time, nature also found how to perpetuate information in those sequences as ancestral AARS genes.

caption.p: Physico-chemical elements relevant to the origins of AARS reflexivity. a) Transfer free energies for amino acids and ribonucleotide base side chains. The y axis is the free energy for transferring the side chain from vapor to cyclohexane. It is thus a surrogate for size. The x axis is the corresponding free energy for transferring the side chain from water to cyclohexane. It is a surrogate for polarity. The plot thus compares the physical chemistry of the nucleic acid and protein alphabets. Class I amino acids are dots; Class II amino acids are squares. Class I amino acids are predominantly bigger and span a larger range of polarity. Diamonds show mean (solid) and median (outline) for each class. b) Schematic of the role of a putative ancestral bidirectional AARS gene in the information flow in genetics. A bidirectional ancestral gene and its mRNA transcripts are inside the gray panel. The gene is a bidirectional gene encoding Class I and II AARS on opposite strands. The respective translated peptides are written with a binary alphabet, with amino acids A and B activated by Class I and Class II synthetases, respectively. Acylated RNAs are shown as capital letters linked to a ellipses representing the symbolic codon representation. A folded conformation is essential for recognition of both amino acid and RNA substrates and for stabilizing the two transition states for carboxyl group activation and acyl transfer. Paired cycles of large arrows define the reflexivity of AARS within each class. Supplies of building blocks (acylated RNAs within amber ellipses) must be created by the two proteins, which must fold to catalyze the crucial reactions. Selection and gene replication are implicit in the cycle labeled “transcription.”

sec.p: Converting the information in genes into proteins vastly expands it. The standard nucleic acid alphabet has only four letters, and all four letters have almost the same physical properties (Fig. 1a). They also have almost the same polarity. Because both scales in Fig. 1a are both logarithmic, the 20 (smaller) amino acids span a vastly greater range of both size and polarity. Thus, the structural and chemical roles of amino acids are far more diverse than those of the four bases. They are also larger and thus cannot pack as tightly into active sites as can amino acids. We have estimated on that basis that chemical engineering by proteins is roughly a billion times more diverse and proficient than that by ribozymes (See Supplementary material in Carter and Wills 2018b). For that reason, the invention of coding signals a major advance in life's agency.

AARS come in two different evolutionary Classes (Eriani et al. 1990). Their respective specificities are shown in Fig. 1a. That partition has long puzzled the field. Many have tried to rationalize or explain the partition (Delarue and Moras 1992; Cusack 1994; Rodin and Ohno 1995; Ribas de Pouplana and Schimmel 2001a ; Klipcan and Safro 2004; Rodin and Rodin 2006; Delarue 2007; Safro and Klipcan 2013; Takénaka and Moras 2020). We consider the class partition to be an essential and penetrating clue to how genetic coding emerged, as outlined next.

sec.title: The Earliest Synthetase Genes Encoded Class I and II Synthetases on Opposite Strands
sec.p: Rodin and Ohno (1995) proposed that the first Class I and II genes arose on opposite strands of the same nucleic acid. They saw that antiparallel coding sequences for conserved “signature sequences” defining the two classes have far more base pairing than expected for random alignments. Three orthogonal kinds of evidence validate the hypothesis: (i) The complementary regions do indeed encode catalytically active synthetases AARS (Carter et al. 2014; Carter 2015). (ii) Independently reconstructed ancestral Class I and II sequences show increasing base pairing frequencies as they approach the root of the tree of life (Chandrasekaran et al. 2013 ). (iii) Bidirectional genes have been designed and expressed. Translated products from both strands have the same catalytic proficiency, within experimental error, as the native sequences (Martinez-Rodriguez et al. 2015; Onodera et al. 2021). We show below that the Rodin–Ohno hypothesis has extraordinary retrodictive power.

Several facets of bidirectional coding merit further comment. The prevailing view (Crick 1970) holds that only one strand does the coding for a given protein; the other strand is simply a template for reconstructing the coding strand. Exceptions to this rule are widespread in viruses, and in general, it is also true that both strands carry coding genes in higher organisms (Wright et al. 2022). But to our knowledge, bidirectional in-frame genes for different proteins are extremely rare and controversial (LéJohn et al. 1994a, 1994b; Yang and LéJohn 1994) in contemporary molecular biology.

Moreover, in-frame bidirectional coding suggests a contradiction. The inversion symmetry of base pairing means that either strand can be reconstructed if you know the sequence of the other. Thus, only one strand of a gene carries unique sequence information. How, then, can a bidirectional gene encode two radically different 3D protein structures? We explore that question in the next section. Second, unlike all but one (Wong et al. 2016; Takénaka and Moras 2020) of the prevailing (Crick et al. 1976; Carter 2008; Yarus et al. 2009; Koonin and Novozhilov 2017) ideas about origins of coding, the Rodin–Ohno hypothesis serves us metaphorically, as an attractor (Newman 1996). It keeps spawning new, testable predictions. Each corroboration enriches the narrative.

sec.title: A Curious Inversion Symmetry in the Genetic Coding Table Projects Base Pairing into the Proteome
sec.p: Zull and Smith (1990) observed that codon assignments are consistent with projecting the nucleic acid inversion symmetry into the proteome (Fig. 2). Genetic codons are assigned to amino acids such that all 14 codons for large, nonpolar residues are anticodons for highly polar residues. Dipeptides that define turns (i.e. Pro-Gly; Gly-Pro) work in both directions on opposite strands. Sequence logos for a set of bidirectional urzyme genes (Fig. 2a) illustrate the variety of sequences enabled by that detail of the coding table. The mean residue-by-residue transfer free energy for water to cyclohexane partition of amino acid side chains for that alignment, <ΔGw > chx>, reveals a near perfect reflection symmetry in antiparallel plots for the 93 amino acids in the designed bidirectional urzyme gene alignments (Fig. 2b).

caption.p: Inversion symmetry of base pairing projects into the proteome. a) Sequence logos (Crooks et al. 2004) from a set of designed bidirectional genes constrained by an active TrpAC and HisCA urzymes both of which have 93 residues. b) Plots of <ΔGw > c>, the free energy of transfer from water to cyclohexane for each sidechain in the sequence, an experimental metric for the polarity of the sidechain. Sequences of Class I AARS (blue) are plotted left to right; those for Class II (red) are plotted right to left. c) Consensus class-defining signature sequences from Class I LeuAC (1WZ2) and Class II HisCA (1HTT) show the impact of the inversion symmetry in the translated proteins. Class I signatures, H(V)GH and K(M)SKS, include mostly polar residues with key catalytic functions. Class II signatures, Motifs 1 and 2, form extensive nonpolar interactions.

sec.p: The reflection symmetry of bidirectional coding has an important consequence for side chain packing and hence the folded conformations of Class I and II AARS. Side chains that form the hydrophobic core in Class I AARS will necessarily form surfaces in Class II AARS. In this sense, the ancestral Class I and II synthetase genes folded up inside out.

That relationship affects the two signature sequences in each class (Fig. 2c). The polar Class I signatures, here HV(I)GH and KMSKS, face into the active-site pocket where they accelerate activation and acylation by factors of 105 and 103, respectively, in the full-length enzymes over the acceleration by Class I urzymes (Li and Carter 2013; Tang et al. 2023). The two signatures are linked together only weakly by a nonpolar interaction between the one hydrophobic side chain in each signature. In the absence of the two missing domains, the catalytic contribution of the active site histidine and lysine residues is nearly absent. Their catalytic roles require coupling imposed by domain motions (Li and Carter 2013; Tang et al. 2023 ). By contrast, the Class II signatures Motifs 1 and 2 form a compact nonpolar packing cluster that provides substantial structural stabilization.

sec.title: Inside-Out Folding Retrodicts Class I and II Amino Acid and RNA Substrate Differentiation
sec.p: Class I and II protozymes provide the AARS ATP-binding determinants. When superimposed on their adenosine moieties (Fig. 3a and b), we see that their α-phosphates are prochiral, hence offering nonequivalent environments. The amino acids react from opposite sides. Class I side chains, which for related amino acids are always bigger, have more room to grow because they face away from the protein (Fig. 3a). Class II side chains are smaller; they have less room because they would grow into the protein (Fig. 3b).

caption.p: Inside-out folding accounts for both amino acid specificity and tRNA groove recognition. a and b) Superposition of the adenosine moiety of Class I ValRS and Class II ThrRS protozymes reveals that the α-phosphate and aminoacyl groups in the amino acid activation sites are nonidentical (near) stereoisomers because the protein portion is on the same side, whereas the amino acids are acylated to different faces of the 5'–phosphate. Thus, they are diastereroisomeric. The loci of activated amino acids of valine a) and threonine b) face in opposite directions, accounting for the observation that Class I side chains are uniformly larger than homologous Class II side chains. c and d) Class I and II AARS recognize RNA substrates from opposite grooves. The 3′-acceptor stem forms a tight RNA hairpin (red rectangle) in Class I RNA substrates. The N-terminus of the Class I specificity-determining helix forms extensive interactions with that hairpin as indicated by dashed lines between protein positively charged groups and negatively charged phosphate groups in the RNA and nonpolar interactions between a protein aromatic residue and the nonpolar face of the A76 ribose (matching green blocks). Class II RNA 3′-acceptor stems are extended, leaving the initial base pair of the stem to form a platform for recognition by the Motif 2 protein hairpin (red rectangle).

sec.title: Inside-Out Folding Assures that Class I and II Synthetases Bind to Opposite tRNA Grooves
sec.p: A similar asymmetry differentiates tRNA binding by Class I and II AARS. The distinctly different tertiary folds of Class I and II AARS necessarily imply that the two classes must use different secondary structural features to complement the shapes of cognate tRNAs. It is not yet possible to understand how the complementary polarity of bidirectional AARS genes dictates their distinctive folds. We can, however, identify some of the more important of those complementary structural distinctions and how they enable tRNA substrate recognition. The 3′ DCCA terminus of Class I tRNA acceptor stems forms a sharp hairpin. That RNA hairpin is recognized by the N-terminus of the specificity-determining α-helix in Class I urzymes. Class II acceptor stems are extended, leaving the initial base pair of the nucleic acid helix as a platform for the Motif 2 protein hairpin. Thus, Class I uses a protein helix N-terminus to recognize a phosphate group exposed by the 3′-terminal hairpins. Class II uses a protein hairpin to recognize the terminal base pair of the tRNA acceptor stems. The synthetases for aromatic subclasses Ic (Trp, Tyr) and IIc (Phe) are exceptions (Ribas de Pouplana and Schimmel 2001b).

The duality of amino acid recognition combines with that of the RNA recognition mechanism. The 2D coordinate system in Fig. 3a and c versus b and d underlies the initial discriminations allowing ancestral AARS and their RNA substrates to enforce a rudimentary coding alphabet. The distinctions between the signature sequences in Fig. 2c and the specificities defined in Fig. 3 reinforce the idea that the base pairing inversion that is fundamental to heredity also projects into the proteome to define substrate recognition by ancestral AARS. We discuss the alphabets that might have grown from that projection in more quantitative detail in a subsequent section.

sec.title: AARS Urzymes and Protozymes Are More Than Simple Analytical Constructs
sec.p: We coined the terms “urzyme” and “protozyme” to suggest that the constructs are models for ancestral AARS forms. That notion implies that they may resemble evolutionary intermediates. We argue that their catalysis and substrate specificities are appropriate to have helped launch genetics. Despite an ample literature, skepticism remains about their authenticity. Only one other group has tested any of our biochemical work (Onodera et al. 2021). In light of such lingering doubts, this section describes three recent studies suggesting to us that the nested hierarchies may represent more than meets the eye. The first entails the likely prior ancestry of the ATP-binding moieties. A second entails an urzyme for a new Class II AARS from an annotated eukaryotic genomic database (Patra et al. 2024). The third describes how E. coli generates a similar nested hierarchy of active segments.

sec.title: AARS Protozymes Were Among the Earliest Peptide Catalysts
sec.p: The protozyme is a module with around 50 amino acids that occurs at the N-terminus of Class I AARS and at the C-terminus of Class II urzymes. We have advanced two reasons why the protozyme was the earliest module of the ancient AARS. First, both Class I and II protozymes contain the respective ATP-binding sites. They speed ATP-dependent amino acid activation up about a million fold (Martinez-Rodriguez et al. 2015). Thus, they catalyze the most important kinetic barrier to protein synthesis. There is also phylogenetic evidence that the protozyme is the oldest sequence in Class I AARS (Carter et al. 2022 ).

We can place the appearance of AARS protozymes as follows. Recent evidence (Moody et al. 2024) suggests that the last universal common ancestor (LUCA) lived ∼4.2 billion years ago. It had a modern metabolism. Phylogenetic evidence (Fournier et al. 2011; Fournier and Alm 2015) suggests that the AARS had acquired their modern lengths before LUCA. Prebiotic chemistry, on the other hand, dates from the time when liquid water first appeared on earth, perhaps a million years or so before LUCA. These dates suggest that protozymes likely appeared after a somewhat extended period of bio-organic chemistry but before the appearance of cells. That puts the entire evolution of the genetic coding system within the error bars of the various dates.

sec.title: AARS Urzymes Occur in Annotated Genomic Databases
sec.p: While assembling the aars.online database (Douglas et al. 2024c), one of us (J.D.) noted that the Arctic Fox genome has a gene for the α-subunit from the bacterium Streptococcus alactolyticus GlyRS-B. That curious orphan gene is a quirky result either of horizontal gene transfer or contamination in the eukaryotic Vulpes lagopus genomic data entry. The GlyRS-B clade is found only in bacteria and chloroplasts. It is an unusual heterotetramer in which tRNAGly binding is relegated to an idiosyncratic β-subunit with homology not to other AARS, but rather to four other cellular proteins with RNA binding functions (Han et al. 2023 ). Only the α-subunit is present in the genome. It is not present in genomes from related foxes.

Two purine base insertions create a potentially functional intron in the annotated gene, separating it into two ORFs (Fig. 4a). AlphaFold2 predicts that ORF1 has the same structure as the smallest Class II synthetase urzyme derived from HisRS. A crystal structure for the E. coli GlyRS-B confirms the AlphaFold prediction (Han et al. 2023). We expressed constructs with a shortened ORF1 and combining ORF1 with ORF2 (Fig. 4b). Both these excerpts accelerate aminoacylation of tRNAGly (albeit with high KM values). The GlyCA glycine activation rate acceleration is intermediate between those of Class II HisCA and Class I TrpAC and LeuAC urzymes (Patra et al. 2024).

caption.p: GlyRS urzymes derived from the V. lagopus genomic database (adapted from Patra et al. 2024). a) Schematic of the annotated genomic entry for the Arctic Fox GlyRS-B α-subunit. Two inserted purine bases (top vertical arrows) create frameshifting (bottom vertical arrows) and an internal stop codon that produces two ORFs. ORF1 ends with a stop codon C-terminal to the red frameshifted sequence. b) These differences change the modularity of the V. lagopus GlyRS α-chain. 3D structures to the right are AlphaFold2 predictions for both ORFs. Colors are those used in the schematic. AlphaFold2 prediction matches closely that observed in PDB ID 7YSE (Han et al. 2023). That allows visualization of likely binding geometry for glycine-5 sulfoamyl adenylate (spheres). The annotated intron coding sequence is frameshifted and corresponds to what is called the insertion domain C-terminal to the active site. Motif 3 and an internal helix-turn-helix motif that covers the terminal adenosine in HisCA2 (Li et al. 2011) are thus missing in both GlyCA1 and GlyCA1 to 2 (created using PyMOL, Pymol 2010). c) Comparisons of a Coomassie stained native gel (right) to a zymogram visualized in situ using absorption of the malachite green orthophosphate complex by GlyCA and GlyCA1 to 2 (Cestari and Stuart 2013).

sec.p: Figure 4c shows zymograms for both constructs with accompanying Coomassie-stained native PAGE gels. These zymograms afford new, visual evidence that the catalytic activity migrates with the major band.

sec.title: Escherichia coli Makes In Vivo the Same Nested Hierarchy of Synthetase Protozymes and Urzymes the Construction of Which Required Two Decades of Analytical Work
sec.p: We designed the LeuAC urzyme by stitching together the conserved cores containing the HI(V)GH and KMSKS signatures and deleting the connecting peptide and anticodon-binding domains. One of us (G.Q.T.) mutated the HVGH and KMSKS signatures to AVGA and AMSAS, thereby making a double mutant of full-length native LeuRS for use in an earlier publication (Tang et al. 2023). Notably, this experiment was intended as a straightforward mutagenesis for that work. It was neither done under selective pressure nor was it a directed evolution experiment.

A multi-round PCR procedure using a megaprimer (Picard et al. 1994) containing the full-length Pyrococcus horikoshii LeuRS gene helped to assure that only full-length plasmids were used in transforming a RecA− strain of E. coli. Despite these precautions, only ∼60% of the transformed E. coli colonies had the expected full-length double mutant plasmid (Fig. 5a). The remaining plasmids all had deletions of almost exactly those variable regions we had deleted in vitro (Fig. 5b).

caption.p: Escherichia coli provides an in vivo system to track modular evolution of AARS. a) Simplified scheme for mutating both HVGH and KMSKS signatures using bidirectional mutagenic primers. b) Schematic representation of plasmids sequenced from single colonies isolated after transforming E. coli with the full-length double mutant (AVGA/AMSAS) LeuRS plasmid. Most recombinant deletions preserve the exact sequences from the input full-length double mutant plasmid but are missing extended fragments from the LeuAC urzyme. c) Segments of Clone22, the “urzyme-like” deletion. It retains the second crossover connection (second Xvr) but is missing the C-terminal β-strand of the protozyme. It must fold differently from the urzyme because of the deletions, as indicated by the dashed arrow. d) Segments of Clone21, the “Goldilocks” urzyme so named because it is just the right length. Deletion of the specificity helix and of most of the second crossover connection from the Rossmann dinucleotide-binding fold means that this construct folds differently than either Clone 22 or LeuAC, because the green segment containing the KMSKS loop is joined directly to the protozyme (dashed line). The 3D structures for c) and d) are taken from the crystal structure 1WZ2 of the P. horikoshii LeuRS. e) Steady-state kinetic parameters for aminoacylation of minihelix by wild-type and double mutant (DBL) versions of Clone 21 and Clone 22 compared with those previously published for the corresponding LeuAC variants. f) Five 3D structures predicted by Alfafold3 (Abramson et al. 2024) for the mRNA of full-length P. horikoshii LeuRS have been aligned by superposing the coding region (dark blue) for the KMSKS-bearing loop (green segment in c).

sec.p: The recombinant deletions were not observed when we transformed E. coli with either single-site mutant or when we tried to reverse the active site deletions using wild-type primers and the double mutant LeuRS plasmid. Together with our use of the megaprimer method, these controls effectively rule out the possibility that the recombination occurs during PCR. Thus, it is highly likely that these deletions are formed in vivo in response to the toxicity of the double mutant plasmid. It remains an open question whether or not that toxicity can be related to the potential orthogonality of the archaeal and bacterial LeuRS genes (Anderson and Schultz 2003). What is relevant here is that the deletions compose a discrete, homogeneous set that mimics what we did intentionally.

What is most surprising about the recovered sequences is that they form an unusually discrete, coherent subset (Tang and Carter 2025). Shorter genes bearing the two longer deletions retained both mutant signatures. Both recombinants Clone 22 (Fig. 5c) and Clone 21 (Fig. 5d) that retain the AMSAS sequence are active leucyl-tRNA synthetase urzymes (Fig. 5e). The rest of the recombinant deletions were protozymes.

The catalytic activities summarized in Fig. 5e were measured using saturating concentrations of isoleucine and ATP as described in Tang et al. (2023). Readers are directed to that paper for information about how these values compare with those for the full-length P. horikoshii LeuRS. The comparison is complicated by the fact that the minihelix is so much better a substrate than full-length tRNALeu for all the urzymes, including the wild-type and double mutant (DBL) Clone 21 and 22 recombinant deletions.

sec.title: The Goldilocks Deletion Maps to 3D Structure in Its mRNA
sec.p: The intermediate Goldilocks deletion Clone21 is especially interesting because although it retains the AMSAS sequence, it is missing most of the second crossover connection of the Rossmann dinucleotide fold (dashed line in Fig. 5f). Its structure is unknown but must differ substantially from that of the corresponding sequences in the full-length LeuRS (Fig. 5d). It represents the addition to the protozyme of the mutant AMSAS signature.

Notably, that short fragment seems to be both necessary and sufficient to enable catalysis of acyl transfer from the activated aminoacyl-5′AMP to a substrate RNA. Further, as noted in Figs. 2a and 7, it is the same length as the Class II HisCA urzyme. That parity appears also to resolve the challenge of designing Rodin–Ohno bidirectional urzyme genes. For these reasons, the Goldilocks urzyme may also represent the minimal evolutionary path from protozyme to urzyme.

The discrete locations and high reproducibility of these deletions strongly suggests that they result from 3D structural properties of the gene itself. We had suggested as much in our initial paper describing the TrpRS urzyme (Pham et al. 2007). To investigate that possibility, we predicted mRNA structures using AlphaFold3 (Abramson et al. 2024). AlphaFold3 has only rudimentary capability to predict RNA structures and the absence of many training data sets. As a consequence, predicted structures are highly variable. Nonetheless, one long stem-loop was present in each prediction (Fig. 5f). That loop contains the exact codons for the module that converts the LeuRS protozyme into the Goldilocks urzyme. That coincidence is consistent with the hypothesis that the nested recombinant deletions in Fig. 5b and by implication the synthetase modularity arise from structures within the mRNA that promote recombination. In vivo models of this sort could be an invaluable tool to study the enzymology of modular protein evolution (Fig. 5e).

sec.title: We Need to Adapt Phylogenetics to Address the Unique Problems Posed by Synthetases
sec.p: Synthetases pose four problems for conventional phylogenetics algorithms: Each requires a qualitatively different innovation in phylogenetic software. Our pursuit of more reliable ancestral reconstructions made it necessary to implement solutions to each of these problems. We discuss these briefly in this section.

sec.title: AARS Exhibit Numerous, Diverse Insertions and Deletions
sec.p: Indeed, both superfamilies likely grew to their contemporary form from quite small protozymic ancestors largely by assimilating modular bits of genetic information (Fig. 6). The gradual accumulation of insertion modules on the enzyme surface cannot be treated adequately using amino acid substitution models alone (Le and Gascuel 2008). An additional Bayesian probability model is required to accommodate the birth and death of insertion modules over long evolutionary timescales in both AARS superfamilies.

caption.p: Insertion elements at the root of the AARS family trees (adapted from aars.online; Whelan and Goldman 2001; Douglas et al. 2024c). Schematic representation of two extant AARS (color) in the context of putative ancestors (black and white). The succession shown here is truncated to include only the transition from protozyme to urzyme to catalytic domain. The purpose is to illustrate how important modular accretion was to the evolution of both AARS superfamilies. Note the succession converting ancestral (black) to more modern (white) secondary structures. Modular accretion continued to occur throughout the evolution of both superfamilies (see Figure 4 of Douglas et al. 2024a).

sec.p: We addressed this problem in two stages. First, we created an online database with access to data on secondary and tertiary structure alignments for both superfamilies (Douglas et al. 2024c). Many authors in the field contributed to this database, which now provides more rigorously curated alignments. That database helped us to identify how indels differ systematically from one synthetase to another. The second stage was to build a Bayesian probability and a Dollo birth/death model for BEAST2 (Douglas et al. 2024a ).

The earlier work (Douglas et al. 2024a) established the unexpected conclusion that AARS for the most complex amino acids, tryptophan and tyrosine, resemble the oldest synthetases (Fig. 6). TrpRS and TyrRS appear to be the most recent additions to the Class I superfamily (Ribas de Pouplana et al. 1996). TrpRS and TyrRS also are the smallest of all AARS and also have the simplest modularity (Doublié et al. 1995). Thus, they also seem to be the most ancient. Occam's razor suggests it is unlikely that earlier versions had additional modules that all were lost in order to enhance their specificity. More likely, a simpler dormant ancestral gene proved a better choice when amounts of the two amino acids increased to the point where it became practical to create a new coding letter, as suggested in Fig. 6. We called such a case “retrofunctionalization” to be compared with “neofunctionalization” and “parafunctionalization” (Lynch and Force 2000; Rastogi and Liberles 2005). Other examples likely occur in both superfamilies.

sec.title: AARS Sequence Evolution Likely Entailed Substantial Saltation
sec.p: One of the most problematic aspects of building reliable trees is having to assume a gradualistic clock-like mutation process. Yet, branching often appears to entail multiple correlated changes (Katsnelson et al. 2019). Localized spikes of evolutionary change have been well-documented experimentally for some more recent protein superfamilies (Bridgham et al. 2009; Manceau et al. 2020). Such cases were summarized earlier at the macromolecular level (Eldredge and Gould 1972). Various attempts to solve this problem have had gradually improving success (Bokma 2002; Pagel et al. 2006; Manceau et al. 2020).

Consistent probabilistic treatment of rapid changes with a birth/death model (Douglas et al. 2025b) led to several substantial improvements. We assumed that each lineage experiences a rapid evolutionary spike, whose size is informed by the number of unobserved bifurcations along that lineage (i.e. the number of stubs). Branching and evolution do indeed appear to be tightly coupled. Despite the model's high dimensionality, several results convince us that it has many attractive properties. Most important are enhanced statistical consistency and low covariation between rates and spikes. The model consistently recovers correct known models from simulated data.

Unobserved speciation events do seem to have left their footprints on the lineages that have been observed. We hypothesized that abrupt evolution could occur at any level in biology, demonstrating the process in genes, morphologies, and languages (Douglas et al. 2025b). Identifying them produced more accurate divergence times. They also resulted in more reliable branchpoints. We demonstrated these enhancements in three detailed case studies spanning the entire spectrum of phylogenies. AARS molecular sequences, morphological changes in cephalopods, and social evolution of Indo-European cognate word phylogenies all exhibit different behavior. In particular, AARS branchpoints are indeed associated with highly elevated fixation of point mutants.

sec.title: The Amino Acid Substitution Matrix Must Be Updated Dynamically
sec.p: Standard amino acid substitution models rely on the critical assumption that the alphabet of amino acids remained constant through time (Whelan and Goldman 2001; Le and Gascuel 2008). While this assumption might hold for younger proteins (e.g. among plants and animals), the assumption becomes increasingly unjustifiable as the age of the phylogeny approaches the LUCA, and even more so dealing with the reflexive properties of the AARS (Carter and Wills 2018b; Shore et al. 2020). This phylogenetic model, under active development, involves the multiplication of a smaller, reduced (n − 1) × (n − 1) amino acid substitution matrix near the root of the tree with the full n × n matrix in the present day, where n is the number of amino acids. Simulation studies can often distinguish subfunctionalization (e.g. IleValRS => IleRS + ValRS) from neofunctionalization (IleRS => IleRS + ValRS). A description of this algorithm has quite significant discriminatory power when applied to the (n = 20), (n-1 = 19) case (Douglas et al. 2025a).

sec.title: Class I and II AARS Trees Must Be Coupled
sec.p: A final basic issue for us is how to link the Class I and II AARS trees. Irrespective of the validity of the Rodin–Ohno hypothesis, there is an overwhelming case to be made that even the earliest proteins needed both Class I and II AARS. The essential requirement for protein secondary structure is binary patterns of either polarity and/or size (Serrano et al. 1992; Muñoz and Serrano 1994). Polarity is even more essential for forming 3D structures (Dill and MacCallum 2012; Guseva et al. 2017).

The Rodin–Ohno hypothesis would, if verified, also strongly constrain trees for the two superfamilies. We hoped to infer such a metric from the base pairing frequencies of codon middle bases in the ∼100 antiparallel alignments of Class I versus Class II coding sequences (Chandrasekaran et al. 2013). Codon middle-base pairing between important segments of Class I and II urzyme genes could thus measure the strength of that constraint. Assembling the data needed to compile those statistics, however, is hard owing to the numerous subtle indels within and between different AARS families in both classes.

After puzzling for many years, the Goldilocks LeuAC urzyme (Fig. 5) has told us that the second crossover of the Rossmann fold is not needed for aminoacylation. It was likely a later addition and may not have existed during the brief stage of bidirectional coding. The Goldilocks recombinant provided a crucial datum by revealing that only the short β-strand and KMSKS loop are required for aminoacylation. It has exactly the same length as the recently characterized GlyCA Class II urzyme. That realization reduces our estimate of how long the earliest ancestral AARS were to ∼80 amino acids. Examples from work in progress are shown in Fig. 7.

caption.p: Toward a Rodin–Ohno urzyme gene and a metric to link the early evolution of Class I and II AARS. a) Antiparallel sequence alignments of designed 81-residue Class I and II Rodin–Ohno urzymes based on Class I P. horikoshii LeuRS (1WZ2) and Class II E. coli AspRS (1C0A), with codon middle bases interleaved and colored according to base pairing, as indicated. b) Partial table of codon middle-base pairing frequencies assembled from diagrams similar to that in a). Note in particular the differences in length between the three groups of Class I, II pairs. Also the subclass pairings suggested by middle base pairing frequencies are not as expected (see text). c) Graphic images of the molecular structures implied by the alignments in a). Catalytic residues and both aminoacyl adenylate ligands are shown as sticks. The long, dashed arrow in Class I indicates a counterintuitive covalent bond connecting the C-terminal segment to the protozyme.

sec.p: This preliminary evidence replicates evidence from similar unpublished earlier work done before we found the LeuAC Goldilocks urzyme. It suggests that three ancestral bidirectional genes originally paired Class IA IleRS opposite Class IIB LysRS, Class II ProRS opposite Class I GlnRS, and HisRS opposite TrpRS. Completing the partial table in Fig. 7b may constrain the joint evolution of Class I and II AARS enough to allow us to reconstruct ancestral sequences back to the time when urzymes enforced an earlier code of far fewer than 20 amino acids.

These pairings are surprising because they suggest that wholesale sequence changes accompanied the speciation as the earliest genes became increasingly specific AARS. Four of the putative ancestral sequences shown in Fig. 7b resemble most closely those for “phase 2” amino acids—lysine, glutamine, histidine, and tryptophan—that were not used in the earliest proteins (Wong et al. 2016). Thus, that description should be interpreted to mean that their closest modern descendants, not the original bidirectional genes, coded for proteins with those specificities. The corresponding GlnAC LysAC, HisAC, and TrpAC urzyme sequences thus all seem to be examples of retrofunctionalization (Douglas et al. 2024a).

sec.title: AARS Urzyme•Minihelix Cognate Pairs Are Sufficiently Functional to Launch Genetic Coding
sec.p: The phylogenetic studies outlined in the last section can open the door to more robust sequence distributions for ancestral AARS families. That is only part of the solution to our problem. Any account of survival will rely on relative fitness. The narrative we seek must also account for the survival of the succession of constructs. Proving fitness is a matter of enzymology. Rates and substrate recognition of each successive stage must be consistent with that narrative. This section recaps experiments that test how well AARS urzyme•minihelix cognate pairs might have functioned.

sec.title: Enzymological Studies Confirm that Urzyme•Minihelix Pairs Work Better Than Either Hybrid
sec.p: For decades, the tRNA anticodon was assumed to play the pivotal role in tRNA identity. It does, in ribosomal mapping to mRNA. However, a seminal proposal appeared in 1993 that today's dual-domain synthetase•tRNA cognate pairs grew from a much simpler set of single-domain•minihelix pairs (Schimmel et al. 1993). Urzymes would not be discovered for another decade and a half (Pham et al. 2007; Li et al. 2011). It took two and a half decades to work out details of the operational code. It took more than three decades to try to answer the question: can urzymes acylate not only tRNA but also minihelix substrates?

Schimmel's was a prescient idea, fueled by the fact that several groups had shown that full-length AARS could acylate minihelices that lacked the anticodon (Francklyn and Schimmel 1989; Schimmel 1991; Frugier et al. 1992). The idea that the anticodon and corresponding anticodon-binding domains could have come later also implied that the original basis for tRNA recognition by ancestral synthetases must have depended on a different code embedded in the acceptor stem. The authors proposed no details of how such an operational RNA code might work. Yet, it was the skeleton of the crucial notion that the ancestral coding system might originally have worked with much simpler machines.

For any system to evolve, it must function. Any ancestral system must have been able to perform two tasks with some degree of substrate specificity: activate amino acids and acylate RNA substrates. It is crucial to know whether or not urzymes can recognize amino acids and acylate tRNA minihelices. We have begun to answer this question. Urzymes not only have the capability to acylate cognate minihelices; they actually prefer the minihelix substrate by about 10-fold (Fig. 8a).

caption.p: Evidence for the evolutionary fitness of AARS urzyme•minihelix cognate pairs. a) Comparison of Michaelis–Menten kinetic data for the LeuAC urzyme acylation of TΨC-minihelixLeu and tRNALeu [adapted from Tang et al. (2024)]. b) Amino acid specificity spectra for the LeuAC and GlyCA urzymes [adapted from Patra et al. (2024)]. c) Note in particular that although the Class I and II Urzymes cannot distinguish amino acids as well as full-length AARS, they tend to favor only subsets of amino acids from their own Class. c and d) RNA specificities of LeuAC and GlyCA urzymes. Histograms for the enrichments of aminoacylated sequences over those inferred from the total library for minihelices acylated with Ile and Leu (c; 39 hyperacylated sequences) by AVGA LeuAC mutant and Gly (d; 204 hyperacylated sequences) by GlyCA. Vertical axes are expressed as free energy values in kcal/mole, computed on the basis of the enrichment. Black horizontal lines denote the average values. Dashed lines indicate the free energy differences between cognate and noncognate recognition in each case. Web logos compare the RNA specificity of the AVGA LeuAC mutant and GlyCAenriched fractions. The histograms and web logos represent 10-fold c) and 22-fold hyperacylated d) fractions, respectively.

sec.p: The Michaelis–Menten plot for LeuAC urzyme (Fig. 8a) shows that the Leucine minihelix is an order of magnitude better substrate than tRNALeu. Neither hybrid protein•RNA system works as well (Tang et al. 2024). Thus, if the RNA substrate is a minihelix, the best enzyme to acylate it is the cognate urzyme, and conversely. That antisymmetry is unexpected evidence that the two modules co-evolved from the start of genetic coding. It also tends to confirm the proposal that the minihelix was a legitimate ancestor of modern tRNAs (Möller and Janssen 1992; Di Giulio 1995).

Experimentally, the LeuAC and GlyCA urzymes also differentiate complementary subsets of ∼5 similar amino acids (Fig. 8b). The mean free energy difference between the correct and incorrect amino acid class is about 1 kcal/mole. That means the urzymes mistakenly activate an amino acid from the wrong class about one in five times. That specificity is comparable to what we observed previously (Carter et al. 2014).

The same two urzymes also acylate different subsets from a TΨCminihelix library (Fig. 8c and d). That library contained all combinations of the seven bases at the top of the acceptor stem. Subsets acylated by LeuAC and GyCA were biotinylated and collected on streptavidin beads (Pütz et al. 1997). The selected minihelices were then sequenced, as was the un-acylated library itself. We note from the WebLOGOs that LeuAC recognition relies most heavily on guanine bases in positions 1 and 72. GlyCA, in contrast, depends largely on the Discriminator Base 73.

We show in Fig. 8c and d the distributions of the most hyperacylated minihelices from the library, together with web logos for those distributions. The two urzymes behave differently. LeuAC acylated substantially more minihelices than did GlyCA. The GlyCA urzyme acylated fewer minihelices with correspondingly greater enrichments. It appears somewhat more selective for minihelices from the library, likely because the constant region of the acceptor stem has some residual specificity. The histograms in Fig. 8c and d therefore represent a compromise between showing the same number of enriched sequences and showing the same relative enrichment.

As with the amino acids, cross-specificity is evident. The mean differences in free energies are −0.4 and −1.6 kcal/mole for minihelix specific aminoacylation by LeuAC and GlyCA, respectively. These values are roughly comparable to those observed for the mean differences, ∼1.0 kcal/mole, between Class I and II amino acid substrates.

Here, we begin to disentangle those recognition signals by varying only the seven bases at the top of the acceptor stem. As noted in Methods and Materials, we used the same minihelix library for each urzyme. That library had acceptor stem and TΨC stem-loop sequences derived from P. horikoshii tRNALeu. Our combinatorial library was a minihelix library based entirely on a stem-loop structure of P. horikoshii tRNALeu. It is thus difficult to compare the results in Fig. 8c with the prior expectations based on the catalog of identity elements for recognition of tRNA by cognate AARS (Giegé et al. 1998; Giegé and Eriani 2014).

Discrimination between different tRNAs is a complex problem because the sites recognized by the synthetase are distributed throughout the RNA. It is at present unknown to what extent full-length recognition of cognate tRNAs requires interactions between remote sites on the tRNA. In particular, interactions between the LeuAC urzymes and the anticodon stem loop and the long extra loop used by full-length LeuRS were not possible in ancestral forms. For the moment, then, we prefer not to speculate on how the data in Fig. 8c compare either with the catalog of identity elements or with the rules elaborated in our own work (Carter and Wills 2018a). Mutual recognition by an urzyme•minihelix cognate pair may differ in unexpected ways from that within modern synthetase•tRNA cognate pairs.

As far as we know, these are the only data available so far on the likely fidelity of very early translation systems. They raise this new question: Are these rates and fidelities sufficient to compose a self-consistent reflexive system? Answering that question appears now to be within reach. The phylogenetic software set out in the previous section should enable us to estimate which amino acids were in likely ancestral coding tables. They also should allow us to construct reliable ancestral distributions of sequences for the relevant AARS that might have enforced such ancestral codes. We can then construct combinatorial libraries for those very AARS. We have begun to express and characterize such libraries (Patra et al. 2025). We also have written software for treating the kinetics of catalytic mixtures (Whelan and Goldman 2001; Douglas et al. 2024b). Thus, this experimental platform enables us now to trace the growth of the coding table.

sec.title: What Was the Transition to Ribosome-Catalyzed Polypeptide Assembly Like?
sec.p: It would be remiss of us not to mention ribosomes briefly, as they are the contemporary machine that assembles aminoacylated tRNAs into proteins. The covalent bond between amino acids and tRNAs, however, represent already decoded symbols. Thus, the ribosome, strictly speaking, is not directly involved in genetic coding. What can be said in this context is that the ribosome is massive compared to the components described here that we propose launched template directed protein synthesis. Others have speculated, as we have done for AARS protozymes and urzymes, that the immense size and complexity of the ribosome itself must have had much simpler ancestors (Petrov and Williams 2015; Bose et al. 2022; Kawabata et al. 2022). Even the proto Peptidyl Transferase Centers or PTCs described by those authors are complex enough that they, too, may be advanced stages of the early evolution of the ribosome. That poses a key question: What functional advantages selected the proto PTC from among preexisting nucleic acids in the first place?

sec.title: Reciprocally Coupled Gating Suggests the Existence of Two, New, Biological Forces
sec.p: The reason there are so many different attempts to define the origin of life is that life itself seems so highly improbable. For life to emerge on early post-Hadean earth many, quite different things all had to happen at almost the same time. Translation was only one of the most important and challenging of them. It could have emerged only under quite special circumstances. Those include a steady input of free energy (Liu et al. 2020), rudimentary metabolism (Sobotta et al. 2020 ; Stubbs et al. 2020), mutualism relating polypeptides and nucleic acids (Lanier et al. 2017), and possibly some sort of compartmentation (Zhu et al. 2013). These areas are outside the scope of this work, but they all must have been provided in some form.

Genetic coding alone seems, at first glance, to be highly improbable. After all, within the vast combinatorial space of amino acid-to-codon assignments and gene sequences, only a tiny fraction can enforce reflexive genetic coding. With more than 1084 ways to assign 64 codons to one or more of the 20 amino acids and >10105 possible amino acid sequences that are the length of an urzyme, an exhaustive search for suitable subsets of assignments and genes would exceed the resources of the known universe. How then did a suitable combination emerge here on earth?

Such combinatorial problems abound almost anywhere one looks; they are especially common to biology. They can be described as “Levinthal-like” paradoxes because they share the formulation articulated by Levinthal for protein folding (Levinthal 1968; Dill and Chan 1997). Solutions to that paradox take the general form of a free energy landscape or “funnel” (Socci et al. 1998). The idea of a funnel is that the gradient of the free energy surface guides the search through combinatorial space by making changes favorable if they move toward a free energy minimum. An analogous fitness landscape likely guided the search for suitable combinations of amino acid-to-codon matching and genes.

What might have shaped such a funnel in the fitness landscape on which genetic coding emerged? A force is a change in an energy field that changes the direction or velocity of motion. If we view evolution as motion on a landscape, then selective constraints imposed on that motion can be seen as biological “forces.” One selective constraint is that a translated gene sequence can affect chemistry best if and only if it always folds into about the same 3D structure. Another is that a sequence will fold if and only if its amino acids occur in an appropriate order.

These two constraints are shown by the pairs of horizontal red arrows in Fig. 1b. Each acts as a logical gate, reducing the passage either of peptides that do not fold, or of sequences that do not obey the code. The two logical gates can exert an extraordinary, iterative force if the consequent of the first serves as the antecedent of the second and vice versa. We argued earlier (Wills and Carter 2018; Carter and Wills 2018b) that such coupling makes the launch of coding from protein much more probable than from ribozymal synthetases.

Searching at the same time for the coding table and the gene sequences required to implement it also helps solve the Levinthal paradox by shaping the landscape of evolution. When two logic gates are coupled tip-to-tail with the consequent of one serving as the antecedent for the other, they make a “strange loop” (Hofstadter 1979, 2007). Tip-to-tail coupling creates a self-referential cycle. Computer modeling of autocatalytic sets shows that side reactions known as “parasites” are the chief threat to survival (Takeuchi et al. 2017). Reciprocally coupled gating eliminates parasites twice, once at each gating event.

In chemistry, the spatial gradient of a species' chemical potential induces a change in its equilibrium distribution. Self-reference establishes a threshold beyond which consistent systems become incomplete. Incompleteness, in turn, implies a reservoir of possibilities analogous to the chemical potential gradient. The strange loop thus implies another force. That gradient of novelty drives discovery in ways analogous to the role of chemical potential. Novelty, in turn, is the grist on which selection and evolution work. Searching at the same time for code words (the coding table) and code keys (the AARS gene sequences) adds a cooperative element to the search that enhances its efficiency (Wills and Carter 2018; Carter and Wills 2018b).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: How do the new results in this paper help answer the questions posed in our opening paragraph? We crafted them with some care, because the authors differ on how to view the evolution of agency. Agency offers a helpful shorthand to keep sentences from being too verbose. Too much agency can be conflated with creationism. In the central chapter of his book How Life Works: A User's Guide to the new Biology, Ball (2023) outlines why this question is so vexing. Ball argues that despite the bedrock belief that evolution lacks direction, forethought, and design, goal-oriented phenomena abound in biology. He even suggests that agency itself is what others have missed in attempts to define life.

Here, in a similar vein, we recognize that the question of agency is also intrinsic to our subject. The birth of genetic coding was indeed a trigger of biology's transcendence of chemistry. As such, we must recognize how close to optimal the genetic system really is. Lineages of ever better AARS enzymes built, expanded, and refined a table of symbols to capture the physical chemistry of amino acids. As the table grew, so too did the capacity to enhance the catalytic activity and especially the specificity of the genes whose translated products enforced that growing table. Some property of the first genes allowed that to happen. It is hard to dismiss the significance of this self-guiding process that apparently transformed itself rapidly into the planet's first computational device.

A growing set of new pilot studies help tie the early evolution of AARS to the emergence of genetic coding. Escherichia coli can generate in vivo a nested set of active excerpts similar to the protozymes and urzymes we had previously designed as models for ancestral AARS. A mid-sized “Goldilocks” variant made in vivo and a short new Class II urzyme suggest how to design bidirectional urzyme genes. The inversion symmetry of base pairing in sense/antisense ancestral AARS genes projects into the proteome. That leads in turn to contrasting amino acid and RNA substrate binding modes. Codon middle base pairing may help coordinate the building of Class I and II AARS family trees. Acylation of specific subsets of a minihelix library by Class I and II AARS urzymes shows RNA substrate specificity for the first time. Finally, new phylogenetics routines solve four problems that had blocked rooting AARS trees in reduced coding alphabets. Those algorithms will enhance our experimental work. Together, they form a viable platform to study how nature likely built the earliest genetic coding tables and began to enforce them with quite simple AARS urzyme•minihelix cognate pairs.

sec.title: Methods and Materials
Zymography
sec.p: To visualize amino acid activation in a native polyacrylamide gel chromogenically, zymography was done using a 1.5 mm thick native gel of 8% resolving and 5% stacking devoid of SDS. Protein samples were prepared without adding SDS and β-ME to maintain the native conformation of protein. Prepared protein (50 μg) was then loaded into two separate wells of native gel. Electrophoresis was done with 40 mA steady current at 4 °C. After electrophoresis for 30 min, the gel was transferred from the glass plates for staining. Then, the gel was shaken twice with double distilled water for 5 min. After washing, reaction buffer—50 mM HEPES pH 7.5, 100 mM amino acid (here glycine), 20 mM MgCl2, 50 mM KCl, pyrophosphatase solution (NEB) (0.1 unit/mL), and polyethylene glycol (PEG-8000; Sigma-Aldrich, Cat. No. 25322-68-3) to a final concentration of 5% (w/v)—was poured over the gel. The gel setup was shaken for 45 min at 4 °C. This step ensures complete soaking of substrate mixture into the gel; the low temperature reduces inactivation of the enzyme during perfusion.

After the perfusion, most of the solution was decanted, leaving minimal solution in a static condition at 37 °C. Synthetase activity was activated by adding 5 mM ATP solution dropwise onto the gel surface to cover the whole gel. The gel was incubated for 30 min. After decanting the reaction mix from the gel box, the staining solution (0.05% malachite green in 0.1 N HCl and 5% hexa-ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate solution in 4 N HCl) was added directly onto the gel box. Staining was promoted by shaking for 2 min on a gyro-shaker. The staining solution was made as described in Onodera et al. (2021). The gradual development of green bands (620 nm) around GlyCA protein present in the gel signified the phosphomolybdate-malachite green complex formation and thus the in situ activity of amino acid activation by GlyCA. The gel was photographed using Gel Doc XR+ from Bio-Rad imaging machine.

sec.title: Isolation of Recombinant Deletions from a Plasmid Containing a LeuRS Double Mutant
sec.p: We observed recombinant deletions by sequencing plasmids from ∼90 colonies of E. coli transformed with a double mutant of WT P. horikoshii LeuRS. Histidine and lysine residues of both HVGH and KMSKS catalytic signatures were mutated to alanine for an earlier project (Tang et al. 2023).

sec.title: Identification of In Vivo Recombinant Deletions
sec.p: The plasmid vector was pET11a (Novagen, Sacramento, CA, United States). DNA oligos were ordered from IDT (Integrated DNA Technologies, Coralville, IA, United States). Phusion Plus PCR Master Mix (Cat# F631S) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, United States). Escherichia coli competent cells were partially from Agilent (XL10-Gold ultracompetent cells, Cat# 200315, Santa Clara, CA, United States) and partially using DH5a, homemade, prepared according to the method described by Sharma in 2017. Restriction enzyme DpnI (Cat# 500402, United States) was purchased from Agilent. Purifications and handling of DNA fragment and plasmid used the QIAquick Gel Extraction Kit (Cat# 28704, Qiagen, Hilden, Germany) and QIAprep Spin Miniprep Kit (Cat# 27104, Qiagen, Hilden, Germany) according to instruction, unless stated otherwise.

sec.title: Method for Preparation of Double Mutant Plasmid
sec.p: DNA oligo sequences were designed according to the manual of QuikChange II-E Site-Directed Mutagenesis Kit (Santa Clara, CA, United States) as published online at https://www.agilent.com/cs/library/usermanuals/public/200555.pdf. In line with the oligo DNA design, and for creating the double mutant plasmid DNA, we have implemented a megaprimer PCR method described by Picard et al. (1994).

After DpnI digestion of the PCR mixture at 37 °C from 3 h to overnight according to instruction provided by Agilent (Santa Clara, CA, United States), the DpnI-digested PCR mixture is ready for E. coli transformation. The transformation was done using 0.3 mL DpnI-digested PCR mixture per transformation.

sec.title: Escherichia coli Transformation and Plasmid Sequencing
sec.p: Escherichia coli transformation proceeded according to conventional procedure (Fritsch et al. 1982) and spread onto lysogeny broth (LB) agar plates with antibiotics (ampicillin 50 mg/mL, or carbenicillin 100 mg/mL). After incubation overnight at 37 °C, individual colonies on the LB agar plate were readied for miniprep by following the standard protocol described by QIAprep Spin Miniprep Kit (Cat# 27104, Qiagen, Hilden, Germany).

Plasmid DNA samples were sequenced following standard sequencing sample preparation and submission protocols described by Eton Bioscience (San Diego, CA) online (https://www.genewiz.com/en/Public/Resources/Sample-Submission-Guidelines/Sanger-Sequencing-Sample-Submission-Guidelines/Sample-Preparation#sanger-sequence) with minor modifications. Basically, each sequencing sample was in 15 mL, containing 100 to 200 ng plasmid DNA with 5 to 10 picomole sequencing primer, i.e. seqn1_AVGA or AMSAS_seqn2rv (Tang and Carter 2025).

sec.title: Method for Plasmid Sequencing and Sequencing Analysis
sec.p: Sequence analysis was performed using conventional methods as follows. First, all the DNA sequences were translated into six reading frames of amino acid sequence using Expose translate online at https://web.expasy.org/translate/. For screening the tentative urzyme candidates, each amino acid sequence from six reading frames from a single sequenced plasmid DNA sample was aligned with the sequence of urzyme template using conventional sequencing alignment software such as MAFFT online at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/jdispatcher/msa/mafft?stype=protein (Katoh et al. 2019) or MUSCLE online at https://www.ebi.ac.uk/jdispatcher/msa/muscle?stype=protein (Edgar 2004). The MAFFT output was then curated manually to align homologous segments. Novel plasmids were designated urzyme, urzyme-like, and goldilocks, according to their sequence content, and were collected and subjected for further downstream identification at the enzymology level after protein biochemistry procedures.

sec.title: Aminoacylation of Minihelix Combinatorial Libraries
sec.p: We constructed a minihelix library with sequences 35 nucleotides long derived from the acceptor stem and TΨC stem loop of tRNALeu. We varied only bases 1 to 3 and 70 to 73, and these were given all ∼16.4 K possible combinations. The resulting library includes all sequences of the form 5′P-NNNGGGGCGGGGUUCAAAUCCCCGCCCCNNNNCCA-3′. The acceptor stem of tRNALeu is very GC rich and so will bias recognition by different synthetases.

Minihelix library constructs were generated (Horizon Discovery/Dharmacon) with seven fully randomized positions in the acceptor stem including the first three base pairs and the discriminator base (Schimmel et al. 1993). Minihelix RNA was heated to 90 °C for 2 min and cooled to 22 °C for 10 min. Refolded minihelix RNA was resuspended in acylation buffer: 50 mM HEPES, pH 7.5, 20 mM MgCl2, 20 mM KCl, 5 mM DTT, 20 mM ATP, and 20 mM amino acids. Aminoacylations were performed by mixing the minihelix RNA with a specified urzyme [LeuAC (30 µM) and GlyCA (15 µM), for 30 and 60 min, respectively]. Unreacted amino acids were removed by running each sample through two size exclusion columns (Zeba, 7K, Thermo Fisher). NHS-biotin (Thermo Fisher) was added in excess on ice to attach biotin to any RNA with a primary amine as previously described (Pütz et al. 1997; Chumachenko et al. 2009). Unreacted NHS-biotin was removed by running each sample through two size exclusion columns. Biotinylated RNAs were captured using streptavidin magnetic beads in 0.1 M sodium acetate (pH 5.2) to protect the substrate from degradation. The inactive RNAs were removed by three washes of the beads with sodium acetate. Biotinylated RNAs were released from the beads by breaking the RNA-amino acid bond through incubation in 50 mM HEPES pH 7.5 for 30 min at 37 °C.

After the partitioning steps, short RNA oligonucleotides were ligated to the ends of the active RNAs to facilitate reverse transcription and PCR needed for sequencing using the TruSeq small RNA library prep kit (Illumina). We adapted the ligation steps to 24 h, 15 °C in the presence of 27% PEG 8000 (Promega) to minimize ligation sequence bias, as previously described (Song et al. 2014 ). Duplicate selections and the starting library were multiplexed and sequenced on iSEQ (Illumina), yielding about 1 million sequences per population. The constant regions of the sequences were trimmed off prior to analysis using our python script (Popović et al. 2015). For each population, the filtered reads were counted and compared between populations using FASTAptamer toolkit (Alam et al. 2015).

sec.title: Design of Bidirectional Genes with Class I and II AARS Urzymes on Opposite Strands
sec.p: Initial working models for bidirectional gene construction were performed with PyMOL on grounds suggested by the Leucyl Goldilocks urzyme. The corresponding Class II AARS urzyme was previously thought to be continuous between Motif 1 and Motif 2. The discovery that the second crossover connection of the Rossmann fold is not needed for catalytic activity required some accommodations in the Class II complement because of the overlap between it and the continuous sequence linking Motifs 1 and 2. These accommodations were identified by anchoring the two Class I and II signatures. The Class II N-terminus was then determined by the length of sequence included following the KMSKS sequence in the Class I partner.

The architecture of the Goldilocks LeuAC urzyme (Fig. 5d) provided the template for constructing and testing possible antiparallel alignments. Pymol (2010) representations of candidate urzymes provided the platform for comparing alternative alignments between Class I and II urzymes in which we deleted the second crossover connection. After transferring the sequences saved from PyMOL as .fasta files to Excel and accessing the codon middle bases, we manually counted the number of middle base pairs in a small number of relative antisense alignments. Having established a secure hypothesis from this preliminary study, we assembled three alignments from aars.online (Douglas et al. 2024c). The amino acid sequences were aligned for both candidate classes to the secondary structures as well as the codon middle bases, both of which can be represented by a single letter. We used that platform to assemble multiple sequence alignments, which then were analyzed by an automated procedure to yield a matrix of middle base pairing frequencies.

Structures of the TrpRS (Class I) and HisRS (Class II) urzymes were obtained, and any extant strand breaks were repaired using Rosetta loop modeling (Mandell et al. 2009; https://doi.org/10.1038/nmeth0809-551). To design bidirectional genes, we modified sequence design model ProteinMPNN (Dauparas et al. 2022; https://doi.org/10.1126/science.add2187) to enforce bidirectional genetic coding constraints. To preserve the consensus Class I and II sequence signatures, we fixed various active site residues (Class I: HIGH motif, S5, Q8, P9, A21, H42, M51, D54, K81, K84; Class II: VTDV motif, R86, E87) and designed all remaining positions. We generated 10,000 sequences using the default sampling temperature of 0.1. The code for our modified version of ProteinMPNN capable of designing bidirectional genes can be found at https://github.com/Kuhlman-Lab/proteinmpnn.
